title: PM KUSUM Yojana — Solar Pumps & Power Plants at 60-90% Subsidy
meta_description: PM KUSUM 2026 — get solar water pumps at 60-90% subsidy. 3 components explained: solar plants, standalone pumps, grid-connected. How to apply state-wise.

hero.one_liner: Solar energy for farmers — get solar water pumps at 60-90% subsidy and earn money by selling solar power to the grid

what_is_it: PM KUSUM (Kisan Urja Suraksha evam Utthaan Mahabhiyan) is a solar energy scheme for farmers launched in 2019 by the Ministry of New & Renewable Energy (MNRE). It has three components: (A) Setting up 10,000 MW solar power plants on barren/fallow farmland, (B) Installing standalone solar water pumps of up to 10 HP capacity, and (C) Solarizing existing grid-connected agricultural pumps. The scheme's total budget is ₹34,422 crore with a target of 35.5 GW solar capacity.

The scheme offers 60% to 90% subsidy on solar pump installation — the farmer pays only 10-40% of the cost. For example, a 5 HP solar pump that costs ₹3-4 lakh can be installed for ₹30,000-1,60,000 out of pocket (depending on state and component). The remaining cost is covered by central government subsidy (30%) + state government subsidy (30%) + bank loan (optional).

Component A allows farmers to set up solar power plants (500 kW to 2 MW) on their barren or fallow land and sell the electricity to DISCOMs (power distribution companies) at a pre-agreed tariff. This creates an additional income stream from land that wasn't being used. Farmers earn ₹3-6 lakh per year per MW of solar capacity.

Component B provides standalone off-grid solar pumps for irrigation in areas where grid electricity is unavailable or unreliable. Component C helps existing grid-connected pump users add solar panels — earning money for surplus solar power fed to the grid while powering their pump for free.

eligibility.label: Component A (solar plant)
eligibility.value: Individual farmers, cooperatives, panchayats, farmer producer organizations with barren/fallow land

eligibility.label: Component B (solar pump)
eligibility.value: Individual farmers needing irrigation pumps in off-grid or weak-grid areas. Own land required.

eligibility.label: Component C (solarize pump)
eligibility.value: Farmers with existing grid-connected agricultural pumps who want to add solar panels

eligibility.label: Land requirement
eligibility.value: Farmer must own agricultural land. Component A needs barren/fallow land near electrical substation

eligibility.label: Subsidy split
eligibility.value: Central: 30% + State: 30% + Farmer: 40% (some states cover farmer's share, making it 10-20% out of pocket)

eligibility.label: Application
eligibility.value: Through respective state nodal agencies — each state has its own portal and process. Check with your state MNRE/agriculture department.

extra_sections.heading: Three Components of PM KUSUM Explained
rows: Set up 500 kW-2 MW solar plants on barren land & sell electricity
Install 1-10 HP solar pumps for irrigation in off-grid areas
Add solar panels to existing electric pump, sell excess to grid

extra_sections.heading: Real Cost Breakdown — What Farmers Actually Pay
content:
Example 1: 5 HP Solar Pump (Component B)

Total cost of 5 HP solar pump system: ₹3,50,000. Central government subsidy (30%): ₹1,05,000.
In some states (Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh), the state covers more than 30%, bringing the farmer's share down to just 10-20% (₹35,000-70,000).

Real Savings Over Time

A 5 HP solar pump saves approximately ₹3,000-8,000/month in diesel or electricity costs (depending on usage and local rates). Over 25 years (solar panel lifespan): total savings = ₹10-15 lakh.
The pump pays for itself within 2-4 years, and then provides free irrigation for 20+ years.

Example 2: 1 MW Solar Power Plant (Component A)

Setup cost: ₹1 crore. Subsidy: ₹30L (30% central) + ₹30L (30% state) = ₹60L.
Farmer's share: ₹40L (can take bank loan). Annual electricity generation: 14 lakh units.
Sale at ₹5/unit: ₹70 lakh/year. Payback period: less than 1 year.

steps.title: Check your state's KUSUM portal
steps.description: PM KUSUM is implemented through state nodal agencies. Each state has its own portal and timeline. Search '[Your State] KUSUM Yojana' online. Visit mnre.gov.in/solar/schemes for state-wise contacts and portal links.

steps.title: Register on the state portal
steps.description: Create an account on your state's KUSUM portal. Enter personal details, land details (with 7/12 extract), existing pump details (for Component C), and the type of solar system you want.

steps.title: Submit required documents
steps.description: Upload: Aadhaar, land records (7/12 extract or land title), bank account details, existing electricity bill (for Component C), passport-size photo. Some states also need soil/water test report.

steps.title: Get site evaluation & technical approval
steps.description: State MNRE team conducts a site visit to assess land slope, shade, grid access, and water availability. Technical approval takes 2-4 weeks.

steps.title: Pay your share or arrange bank loan
steps.description: After approval, pay the farmer's share (10-40% of total cost). Banks provide loans at subsidized rates. Some state agencies also facilitate EMI payments. Get three quotes from approved solar vendors.

steps.title: Installation by empanelled vendor
steps.description: An empanelled solar vendor installs the pump/panels. Installation takes 2-4 weeks. After installation, state agency inspects and commissions the system. Warranty: 5 years for pump, 25 years for solar panels.

how_to_apply.title: Check your state's KUSUM portal
how_to_apply.description: PM KUSUM is state-implemented. Each state has different portal, timeline, and additional subsidies.

how_to_apply.title: Register and apply online
how_to_apply.description: Fill application with land details, pump requirements, upload documents.

how_to_apply.title: Pay farmer's share after approval
how_to_apply.description: 10-40% of total cost depending on your state's subsidy structure.

how_to_apply.title: Installation within 2-4 weeks
how_to_apply.description: Empanelled vendor installs. State agency inspects and commissions.

important_alert.text: PM KUSUM subsidy percentages and application processes vary significantly by state. Check your specific state's portal for exact subsidy amounts, current application deadlines, available capacity, and technical requirements. Not all states have opened applications for all components. Phase 2 of PM KUSUM is planned to launch after March 2026.

important_dates.label: Application
important_dates.value: State-wise — check your state's KUSUM portal for current deadlines

faqs.question: How much does a solar pump actually save?
faqs.answer: A 5 HP solar pump saves approximately ₹3,000-8,000/month in diesel or electricity costs (depending on usage and local rates). Over 25 years (solar panel lifespan), total savings are ₹10-15 lakh. The pump pays for itself within 2-4 years, after which all irrigation is essentially free. Plus, you get 5-year warranty on pump and 25-year warranty on solar panels.

faqs.question: Can I sell excess solar power?
faqs.answer: Yes, under Component C (grid-connected solar). If your solar panels generate more electricity than your pump needs, the excess is fed to the grid and you receive payment at the DISCOM's agreed tariff rate (typically ₹5-8/unit). This can earn you ₹500-3,000/month depending on your solar capacity and usage pattern.

faqs.question: My state hasn't opened KUSUM applications. What do I do?
faqs.answer: Not all states have active applications at all times — they open in batches based on budget allocation and implementation capacity. Check your state's renewable energy department website periodically, or register for email notifications. Meanwhile, some states have their own solar pump schemes with similar subsidies — check with your district agriculture office or block-level nodal agency.

faqs.question: What maintenance does a solar pump need?
faqs.answer: Minimal. Solar panels need occasional cleaning (dust/dirt reduces efficiency by 2-5%). The pump motor may need servicing every 2-3 years. There are no fuel costs, no recurring electricity charges. Most installations come with 5-year comprehensive warranty covering pump damage. Solar panel manufacturers provide 25-year performance warranty (guaranteed at least 80% output).

faqs.question: Can tenant farmers or lease farmers apply?
faqs.answer: Generally no — you need to own the land where the solar pump will be installed. However, some states allow it with a long-term lease agreement (10+ years) from the landowner. Check your state's specific eligibility rules on the state KUSUM portal or with your block agriculture officer.

faqs.question: What if I have no connection to the grid?
faqs.answer: That's exactly what Component B (standalone solar pumps) is designed for. If your area has no grid electricity or very weak grid, you can still get a solar pump with 60-90% subsidy. It works entirely off-grid — no electricity bill, no diesel cost, complete independence.

faqs.question: How long does the application process take from start to installation?
faqs.answer: Typical timeline: Registration to approval: 2-4 weeks. Payment by farmer: 1-2 weeks. Installation: 2-4 weeks. Total: 1.5-2.5 months from initial application to working solar pump. Speed depends on state implementation and your document readiness.

ministry: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
